title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
concept: RAG systems search a vector database of sentence embeddings to find relevant documents, appending them as context in the prompt for an LLM to read before generating an answer.

exercises.prompt: Where does a RAG system search to find documents search system look to-the-point RAG system search to find matching documents for matching documents?

options.text: A vector database of sentence embeddings

exercises.explain: A database of sentence embeddings allows the system to compare the meaning of the user's question to the meaning of the stored documents mathematically.

cards.title: AI's Memory Limit
cards.body: AI models are smart, but they don't know your private files or the latest news. How do we feed them the right facts at the right time without retraining them? We use RAG!

cards.title: What is RAG?
cards.body: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique where an AI searches a database of sentence embeddings to find relevant documents, and then pastes that information directly into the prompt so the LLM has the facts it needs to answer.

cards.title: The Open-Book Exam
cards.body: Imagine an open-book exam. Instead of trying to memorize every textbook in the world, you have a super-fast librarian assistant. When a question is asked, the librarian runs to the archives, grabs the exact pages you need, and hands them to you so you can write a perfect answer.

cards.title: RAG in Action
cards.body: If you ask an LLM about your company's secret internal holiday policy, it won't know it. With RAG, the system searches your company's PDF database, finds the "Holiday Policy PDF", and gives that text to the LLM. The LLM then answers you accurately!

cards.title: Myth: RAG changes the AI's brain
cards.body: Many people think RAG retrains or updates the LLM's internal brain. In reality, the LLM stays exactly the same! RAG simply searches for facts and pastes them into the chat window (prompt) for the LLM to read.

cards.title: Standard vs. RAG
cards.body: See how RAG transforms an AI from guessing using old training data to retrieving exact, fresh facts.

right.text: Searches vector database first, reads the documents, and gives a fact-checked answer.

left.text: Guesses answers using only its pre-trained memory. Might hallucinate if it doesn't know.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems?

options.text: To make the LLM generate images instead of text.
To speed up the network connection of the user.
To give the LLM access to external, up-to-date, or private information.
To delete old training data from the LLM.

exercises.explain: RAG acts like an open-book assistant, retrieving relevant facts from a database and placing them into the prompt before generating the final text response.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A RAG system can give an LLM access to new information without having to retrain the LLM.
exercises.explain: RAG works by retrieving external documents and feeding them into the prompt. No training or tuning of the core model is required.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the RAG workflow explanation:
exercises.sentence: To find the best documents, the system searches a vector database of sentence ________.
exercises.explain: RAG uses sentence embeddings to calculate mathematical similarity, helping the system find documents with matching meanings.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI assistants is using a RAG system?

options.text: An AI that writes a fantasy story about dragons using its creative training.
An AI that searches your computer's user manual database to help you fix your specific camera model.
An AI chatbot that translates English greetings into French.

exercises.explain: AI B is using a RAG system because it searches a database to retrieve external files (the manual) to answer the query accurately. AI A and C are relying on pre-existing knowledge.

exercises.prompt: How does a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system actually work under the hood to answer a question?

options.text: It retrains the entire LLM from scratch using your personal text files.
It randomly selects text documents and converts them to image files for the LLM.
It searches vector embeddings to find matching docs, then adds them to the LLM's prompt.
It translates the user's question into programming code to run on a local computer.

exercises.explain: RAG systems first convert the user query and database documents into sentence embeddings (vectors) to search for match relevance. Then, the most relevant documents are retrieved and sent to the LLM as added context so it can generate an accurate response.
